engineIntelligence.h2: Porsche Carrera GT Engines - Codes, Common Failures & Replacement Costs
engineIntelligence.description: The cards below cover the M80.01 5.7L V10 engine fitted to the Porsche Carrera GT in the UK, including documented failure causes, production years, and typical rebuilt engine costs based on current UK specialist market data.

cards.altText: Porsche Carrera GT M80.01 engine - petrol 5.7L replacement UK
knownFailures: Impulse sensor failure (P/N 931 606 021 00) - causes no-start above 4000rpm, documented on early production units.
Ceramic clutch assembly wear - PCCC clutch disintegration requires engine removal for replacement.
Carbon-fibre engine mount cracking - structural fatigue from APA3 recall-related chassis stress.
cards.replacementCost: Typical UK rebuilt engine: £45,000-£120,000 (supply only) Estimated from comparable M80.01 specialist rebuilds - actual quotes may vary due to extreme rarity.

cards.altText: Porsche Carrera GT M80.01 long block engine - petrol 5.7L replacement UK
productionHistory: Porsche Carrera GT (980) long block assembly - 2004-2006
Complete cylinder head and block units - all production years
knownFailures: Timing chain tensioner wear - complex V10 procedure, specialist labour intensive on high-mileage examples.
Dry-sump oil pump failure - external scavenge pump wear affects oil pressure on track-used units.
cards.replacementCost: Typical UK rebuilt engine: £45,000-£120,000 (supply only) Estimated from comparable M80.01 specialist rebuilds - actual quotes may vary due to extreme rarity.

cards.altText: Porsche Carrera GT M80.01 short block engine - petrol 5.7L replacement UK
productionHistory: Porsche Carrera GT (980) short block assembly - 2004-2006
Bottom end crankcase with pistons and rods - all production years
knownFailures: Titanium connecting rod wear - track-focused use accelerates fatigue on high-revving V10 components.
Alusil cylinder liner scoring - requires specialist machining if piston ring failure occurs.
cards.replacementCost: Typical UK rebuilt engine: £45,000-£120,000 (supply only) Estimated from comparable M80.01 specialist rebuilds - actual quotes may vary due to extreme rarity.

commonProblems.h2: Porsche Carrera GT Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: The Porsche Carrera GT's M80.01 V10 is engineering excellence, but age and rarity drive complex replacement decisions. Dominant failure patterns include the impulse sensor (P/N 931 606 021 00), ceramic clutch assembly wear, and carbon-fibre mount fatigue. Every rebuilt M80.01 supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components addressing original failure points - plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty from UK V10 specialists.

problems.h4: Impulse Sensor Failure (P/N 931 606 021 00) - Why Carrera GT Engines Won't Start Above 4000rpm
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Carrera GT (980) all variants, M80.01 V10, 2004-2006
problems.rootCause: In-bellhousing crank reference sensor suffers heat-induced signal loss; unique placement makes access extremely labour-intensive.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove transmission, replace P/N 931 606 021 00 sensor, recalibrate DME
repairOptions.longevity: Temporary fix if sensor is sole fault; rare on high-mileage units

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt V10 with upgraded sensor housing, bench-tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Permanent solution; addresses multiple age-related wear points simultaneously

problems.recommendation: On a Carrera GT valued £400,000+, sensor replacement may suffice if diagnostics confirm isolated failure. However, given the labour overlap with clutch service, we often advise bundling preventative work. For units with additional symptoms, a rebuilt M80.01 offers long-term security.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt M80.01 engine prices for your Porsche Carrera GT

problems.h4: Ceramic Clutch Assembly Failure (PCCC) - The £20k+ Consumable Requiring Engine Removal
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Carrera GT (980) all variants, M80.01 with PCCC clutch, 2004-2006
problems.rootCause: Carbon-ceramic clutch discs disintegrate under aggressive use; replacement requires full engine removal due to mid-engine packaging.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove engine, replace PCCC clutch pack, flywheel, release bearing; reinstall and calibrate
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if engine internals are sound; labour dominates cost

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt V10 with new PCCC clutch assembly, upgraded mounts, full warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Best value for high-mileage or track-used cars; eliminates future engine anxiety

problems.recommendation: Given the £18k-£32k labour overlap for clutch replacement, if your M80.01 has >15,000 miles or shows any oil consumption, the bundled rebuilt engine + clutch package offers superior long-term value and resale confidence.
problems.cta: Get quotes for Carrera GT engine and clutch replacement package

problems.h4: Carbon-Fibre Engine Mount Failure - APA3 Recall Impact on M80.01 Bay Access
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Carrera GT (980) all variants, M80.01, 2004-2006 (APA3 recall-affected chassis)
problems.rootCause: Carbon-fibre monocoque mount points develop micro-fractures under suspension recall-related chassis flex; compromises engine alignment and bay access.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove engine, repair/replace carbon mount points, realign subframe, reinstall engine
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if mounts are sole issue; requires specialist carbon-fibre repair capability

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt M80.01 + Mount Upgrade
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt V10 with upgraded mount hardware, chassis reinforcement, full warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Addresses root cause; recommended for recall-affected chassis with >15k miles

problems.recommendation: If your Carrera GT is APA3 recall-affected and shows mount vibration or misalignment, the rebuilt engine + upgraded mount package provides structural peace of mind and preserves collector value.
problems.cta: Compare Porsche Carrera GT engine replacement with mount upgrade options

problems.h4: Dry-Sump Oil System Failure - Pressure Loss on Track-Used M80.01 Units
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Carrera GT (980) track-focused use, M80.01, 2004-2006
problems.rootCause: External scavenge pump wear or oil tank baffle fatigue causes pressure drop under high-G cornering; catastrophic if undetected.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove engine, replace scavenge pump, inspect oil tank baffles, flush system, reinstall
repairOptions.longevity: Suitable if caught early via oil pressure monitoring; requires specialist V10 knowledge

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt M80.01 with Upgraded Dry-Sump
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt V10 with upgraded oil pump, reinforced tank, pressure monitoring, warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended for track-used cars; eliminates dry-sump anxiety permanently

problems.recommendation: For any Carrera GT used on track, invest in continuous oil pressure monitoring. If pressure fluctuations are recorded, the rebuilt engine with upgraded dry-sump components is the only route that protects your £400k+ asset.
problems.cta: Get quotes for track-spec rebuilt M80.01 engine with upgraded oil system

problems.h4: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear - The Silent V10 Killer on High-Mileage Carrera GTs
problems.affectedModels: Porsche Carrera GT (980) high-mileage examples, M80.01, 2004-2006
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 20,000-40,000 miles (V10 complexity accelerates wear vs. flat-six)
problems.rootCause: Complex V10 timing chain routing places high load on tensioners; wear causes chain slap, potential valve contact if ignored.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove engine covers, replace tensioners and guides, reset timing, test run
repairOptions.longevity: Viable only if caught before chain stretch; requires specialist V10 timing expertise

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt M80.01 with Upgraded Timing Components
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit rebuilt V10 with upgraded tensioners, chains, guides, full warranty
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended for any M80.01 >20k miles; eliminates timing anxiety permanently

problems.recommendation: Given the labour overlap with other V10 services, if your Carrera GT is >20k miles or shows any timing-related noise, the rebuilt engine with upgraded timing components is the only route that protects your investment.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt M80.01 engine prices with upgraded timing components

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Porsche Carrera GT
finalCta.paragraph: The M80.01 V10 is a masterpiece, but its complexity and rarity demand specialist care. EngineMarket connects you with UK V10 specialists who understand carbon-fibre integration, dry-sump systems, and ceramic clutch protocols. Every rebuilt engine includes upgraded components addressing original failure points, bench testing, and a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Compare prices from vetted specialists today and protect your £400k+ asset without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Porsche Carrera GT Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Porsche Carrera GT and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

years.description: The inaugural production year introduced the M80.01 V10 with early-spec impulse sensors later subject to field updates.
keyChanges: First UK deliveries of Carrera GT with M80.01 5.7L V10
knownFor: Highest incidence of impulse sensor failure on early VINs
APA3 suspension recall impacts engine bay access on affected units
years.cta: Get quotes for 2004 Porsche Carrera GT replacement engines
years.ticker: Porsche Carrera GT 2004 engine replacement • M80.01 impulse sensor failure • Carrera GT ceramic clutch service

years.description: Mid-production units received minor sensor revisions and improved dry-sump baffle design based on early field feedback.
keyChanges: Revised impulse sensor mounting protocol for improved heat shielding
Dry-sump oil tank baffle reinforcement on later VINs
Ceramic clutch assembly service procedure updated
knownFor: Reduced sensor failure rate vs. 2004 units
Peak production year with highest UK registration volume
Strong collector demand for matching-numbers examples
years.cta: Get quotes for 2005 Porsche Carrera GT replacement engines

years.description: The final production year featured the most refined M80.01 specification with all prior field updates incorporated.
keyChanges: Final-spec impulse sensor with enhanced heat shielding
Last Carrera GT units built before production end (1,270 total)
knownFor: Lowest documented failure rates across all M80.01 production
Highest collector premiums for "last 50" chassis numbers
Strongest resale value retention in the Carrera GT lineage

faq.h2: Porsche Carrera GT Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Porsche Carrera GT engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt M80.01 V10 for the Carrera GT typically costs £45,000-£120,000 supply-only in the UK, depending on specification, core condition and rebuild depth. Supply & fit adds £12,000-£28,000 labour.
keyPoints: Rebuilt M80.01 units range £45k-£120k supply-only
Supply & fit labour adds £12k-£28k depending on chassis condition
Core charge refunds apply when returning your damaged engine
Matching-numbers rebuilds command premium pricing for collectors
items.cta: Compare rebuilt M80.01 engine prices for your Porsche Carrera GT

items.question: What is the most common engine failure on the Porsche Carrera GT?
items.answer: The impulse/reference sensor (P/N 931 606 021 00) is the most documented failure, causing no-start above 4000rpm on early-production M80.01 V10 units.
keyPoints: Sensor P/N 931 606 021 00 suffers heat-induced signal loss
Failure causes no-start above 4000rpm, not total engine seizure
Replacement requires transmission removal due to bellhousing location
Later-production units received revised sensor mounting protocol
items.cta: Get quotes for Porsche Carrera GT impulse sensor replacement

items.question: Can the Porsche Carrera GT ceramic clutch be replaced without removing the engine?
items.answer: No - the mid-engine packaging of the Carrera GT requires full engine removal to access the PCCC ceramic clutch assembly for replacement or service.
keyPoints: PCCC clutch sits between engine and transaxle in mid-engine layout
Engine removal is mandatory for clutch access on all 2004-2006 units
Labour for clutch-only service typically £18k-£32k at UK specialists
Bundled engine + clutch rebuild offers better long-term value
items.cta: Compare Porsche Carrera GT engine and clutch replacement package prices

items.question: Is it worth rebuilding a Porsche Carrera GT engine or buying used?
items.answer: For a £400k+ asset, a rebuilt M80.01 with upgraded components and warranty typically offers superior long-term value versus a used unit of unknown history.
keyPoints: Rebuilt engines include upgraded wear parts and 12-month warranty
Used units carry unknown service history and no upgrade benefits
Collector value favours documented rebuilds over salvage swaps
Core charge refunds reduce effective rebuilt engine cost
items.cta: Compare rebuilt vs used Porsche Carrera GT engine options

items.question: How long does a Porsche Carrera GT engine replacement take?
items.answer: A full M80.01 supply & fit typically takes 3-6 weeks at a UK specialist, depending on engine availability, chassis condition and ancillary work required.
keyPoints: Engine removal and prep: 5-7 working days
Rebuilt engine lead time: 2-4 weeks if not in stock
Installation, calibration and testing: 3-5 working days
Carbon-fibre chassis inspection may extend timeline
items.cta: Get a fixed timeline quote for your Porsche Carrera GT engine replacement

items.question: Does the Porsche Carrera GT engine come with a warranty?
items.answer: Yes - every M80.01 engine supplied through EngineMarket includes a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK V10 specialists, with 24-month options available on select rebuilt units.
keyPoints: Minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty standard
Warranty covers parts and labour at any UK specialist in network
24-month coverage available on premium rebuilt specifications
Matching-numbers rebuilds include documentation warranty
items.cta: View warranty terms for Porsche Carrera GT engine replacements

items.question: What engine code is fitted to the Porsche Carrera GT?
items.answer: The Porsche Carrera GT exclusively uses the M80.01 5.7L naturally aspirated V10 engine code across all 2004-2006 production years and variants.
keyPoints: M80.01 is the sole engine code for all Carrera GT units
Derived from Porsche LMP2000 racing engine heritage
Features dry-sump lubrication and carbon-fibre integration
No other engine codes were ever fitted to UK-market Carrera GTs
items.cta: Get quotes for M80.01 Porsche Carrera GT engine replacement

items.question: Can I fit a later-spec M80.01 engine into my 2004 Porsche Carrera GT?
items.answer: Yes - M80.01 engines are largely interchangeable across 2004-2006 Carrera GT production, though DME calibration and sensor compatibility must be verified by a V10 specialist.
keyPoints: M80.01 block and heads are interchangeable across all years
Later-spec impulse sensors may require DME software update
Carbon-fibre mount points must be inspected for APA3 recall compliance
Specialist calibration required post-installation for optimal performance
items.cta: Compare cross-year Porsche Carrera GT engine swap options

items.question: What oil specification does the Porsche Carrera GT M80.01 require?
items.answer: The M80.01 V10 requires Porsche-approved 10W-60 fully synthetic oil meeting A40 specification, with a dry-sump capacity of 10.5 litres including filter and lines.
keyPoints: Use Porsche A40-approved 10W-60 fully synthetic oil only
Total dry-sump capacity: 10.5 litres including filter and lines
Oil change interval: 10,000 miles or 12 months, whichever comes first
Track use requires pre- and post-session oil level verification
items.cta: Get quotes for Porsche Carrera GT M80.01 servicing and oil supply

items.question: Are Porsche Carrera GT engines reliable for daily driving?
items.answer: The M80.01 V10 is engineered for performance, not daily commuting; reliability depends on specialist maintenance, avoiding short trips, and proactive monitoring of known failure points.
keyPoints: M80.01 is robust when maintained by V10 specialists only
Avoid short trips that prevent oil/system warm-up
Monitor impulse sensor and oil pressure via dedicated gauges
Annual specialist inspection recommended regardless of mileage
items.cta: Find a UK Porsche Carrera GT specialist for preventative maintenance
